title: Jean-Georges Vibert

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Jean-Georges Vibert](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Georges_Vibert&redirect=no "Jean-Georges Vibert"))

French painter (1840–1902)

| Jehan Georges Vibert | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | Jehan Georges Vibert  (1840-09-30)30 September 1840  [Saint-Étienne-de-Saint-Geoirs](/wiki/Saint-%C3%89tienne-de-Saint-Geoirs "Saint-Étienne-de-Saint-Geoirs"), [Isère](/wiki/Is%C3%A8re "Isère"), France |
| Died | 28 July 1902(1902-07-28) (aged 61) |
| Occupation | Academic [painter](/wiki/Painting "Painting") |
| Awards | [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour") - Officer (1882) |

**Jehan Georges Vibert** or **Jean Georges Vibert** (30 September 1840 – 28 July 1902) was a French academic [painter](/wiki/Painting "Painting").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jehan_Georges_Vibert&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Peeping_Roofers_%26_The_Woman%27s_Bath_by_Jehan_Georges_Vibert.jpg/250px-Peeping_Roofers_%26_The_Woman%27s_Bath_by_Jehan_Georges_Vibert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peeping_Roofers_%26_The_Woman%27s_Bath_by_Jehan_Georges_Vibert.jpg)

*Peeping Roofers & the Woman's Bath*, painted in 1880 for the Vanderbilts

He was born in Paris, the son of engraver and publisher Théodore Vibert, and grandson of the influential rose-breeder [Jean-Pierre Vibert](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Vibert "Jean-Pierre Vibert"). He began his artistic training at a young age under the instruction of his maternal grandfather, engraver Jean-Pierre-Marie Jazet. Vibert was more interested in painting than [engraving](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") and entered the studio of [Félix-Joseph Barrias](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix-Joseph_Barrias "Félix-Joseph Barrias") and eventually the [École des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts "École des Beaux-Arts") when he was sixteen. He remained at the École for six years under the instruction of [history painter](/wiki/History_painting "History painting") [François-Edouard Picot](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Edouard_Picot "François-Edouard Picot").

Vibert debuted at the [Salon](/wiki/Paris_Salon "Paris Salon") of 1863 with *La Sieste* (The Siesta) and *Repentir* (Repentance).

During the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"), Vibert became a sharpshooter and was wounded at the battle of [Malmaison](/wiki/Rueil-Malmaison "Rueil-Malmaison") in October 1870.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In recognition of his sacrifice, he was awarded a [Knight](/wiki/Knight "Knight") in France's [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour") on 18 June 1870,[[3]](#cite_note-3) which was upgraded to the [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour") rank of [Officer](/wiki/Officer "Officer") on 18 February 1882.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Vibert submitted work to the Salon until 1899. The popularity of his works spread, notably in America, and fetched high prices including commissions from [John Jacob Astor IV](/wiki/John_Jacob_Astor_IV "John Jacob Astor IV") and [William Vanderbilt](/wiki/William_Kissam_Vanderbilt "William Kissam Vanderbilt"). A large collection of works by Vibert was amassed by the heiress May Louise Maytag on behalf of then bishop of Miami [Coleman Carroll](/wiki/Coleman_Carroll "Coleman Carroll"), who greatly fancied them. This large cache was then donated to the Florida seminary [St. John Vianney College](/wiki/Saint_John_Vianney_Seminary_(Miami) "Saint John Vianney Seminary (Miami)") in Miami. At this location the impressive collection has had a somewhat checkered conservation history, as well as exhibition history due to the discomfiture of later bishops with the seeming [anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clerical_art "Anti-clerical art") of the paintings (lighthearted debaucheries, etc.).

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jehan_Georges_Vibert&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Death")]

Vibert died on 28 July 1902, and is buried at the Père-Lachaise cemetery (4th division) in Paris.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
